Islamic Tradition

Suhrawardī

1154 – 1191

The philosophy of Illumination: light as the metaphysical texture of soul and knowledge.

Here is the reconstruction I think is closest to Suhrawardi’s mature position, especially in the Ḥikmat al-Ishrāq, while still using the Talwīḥāt and Mashāriʿ wa-l-Muṭāraḥāt for developmental context. I am using “philosophy of mind” as a modern umbrella term for a cluster of Suhrawardian themes: soul, self-awareness, perception, imagination, angelic intellect, and divine knowledge. That umbrella is useful, but only if we do not force Suhrawardi into a purely human or secular register. French scholarship on his doctrine of divine knowledge treats that doctrine as a test case for knowledge as such, and Persian scholarship strongly emphasizes that in his mature thought the soul’s epistemic self-presence and its ontological identity are inseparable. So God and the angels are not detachable ornaments here; they belong to the architecture of the theory.

A terminological caution matters at the outset. Suhrawardi does not have one neat word exactly equivalent to the modern “mind.” His core vocabulary includes nafs (soul), ʿaql (intellect), rūḥ (pneuma/spirit), nūr (light), ḥuḍūr (presence), and ishrāq (illumination). A faithful reconstruction therefore has to be layered: human subjectivity, angelic intellection, and divine cognition are not identical, but they are explained through a common logic of luminous manifestation and degrees of unveiled presence.

1. The soul as luminous subject

Suhrawardi’s mature account of the soul is best understood against a real internal development in his corpus. In his more Peripatetic works he can still repeat the Aristotelian-Avicennian definition of soul as a “first perfection” of a natural organic body. But the mature Illuminationist position moves beyond that. Persian scholarship usefully highlights the shift: in the earlier mode the soul’s identity can be cast as sheer anniyya or existence, whereas in the mature Ḥikmat al-Ishrāq its identity is stated as “pure, immaterial light.” The SEP account aligns with this: the soul remains an immaterial, self-subsisting, living, knowing substance that rules the body, but its very substance is now defined in terms of the intensity of its luminosity.

That is not just decorative symbolism. For Suhrawardi, light is the basic ontological notion because nothing is more evident than light and nothing is less in need of definition. Direct knowledge is direct apprehension of the light-nature of things. Persian discussions of his nafs-theory sharpen the point by insisting that the soul’s luminous identity is not added after the fact; it is inferred from the soul’s immediate self-awareness. One non-Anglophone line of interpretation even compares this procedure to a phenomenological description of what is first given, though the comparison must be used carefully because Suhrawardi’s account remains fully metaphysical, not merely descriptive.

A further precision is essential: not every “light” in Suhrawardi is a mind. He distinguishes self-subsistent pure lights from accidental lights such as physical illumination, and from dark barriers or bodies. Pure lights are aware of themselves and of what is other than them; accidental lights are not minds simply because they are luminous. The human soul belongs to the class of self-subsistent pure lights, not to the class of accidental physical lights. That keeps Suhrawardi from collapsing into a simple panpsychism of everything luminous whatsoever.

2. Embodiment without reduction

The human soul is embodied, but not produced by the body and not reducible to it. Suhrawardi describes the relation between the luminous soul and the dark body in terms of dominion (qahr) together with love or desire (maḥabba, ʿishq). Between them stands the subtle psychic pneuma (rūḥ), which functions as an intermediary capable of receiving images, forms, or icons and manifesting them to the soul. This is already enough to show that Suhrawardi is not an indiscriminate eliminativist about mental imagery. What he resists is the claim that copied forms inhering in material organs are the basic explanation of cognition.

So the real subject of cognition is the soul, not the organ. Persian discussion of Suhrawardi’s sensory theory presses this point nicely: the soul has presential awareness of the body and its faculties, and when an illuminated object stands before the eye, the soul comes to the “station” of the eye and itself becomes the real seer. The organ is a condition and a locus of manifestation, not the ultimate knower. This makes Suhrawardi’s mature view neither ordinary Aristotelian hylomorphism nor early modern mind-body dualism in the Cartesian mold. It is a hierarchical relation of governance, mediation, and manifestation.

3. Consciousness, selfhood, and presence

At the center of Suhrawardi’s philosophy of mind is self-awareness. Self-awareness is immediate, direct, and unmediated by any form, trace, impression, concept, or attribute added to the self. He treats pain as a paradigm case of non-discursive awareness, and the same structure applies to the awareness by which the self knows itself as “I.” The SEP entry stresses that this awareness is prior to subject-object division, while Persian work emphasizes that the self is not absent from itself across waking, sleep, sobriety, and even intoxication. This is why self-awareness is not merely one mental act among others; it is the primitive fact from which the ontology of the soul is read off.

This is also why “knowledge by presence” can sound a little too instrumental. Kaukua explicitly notes that “knowledge as presence” is often the better rendering, and a later Brill summary makes the same point in stronger form: knowledge is not merely obtained by means of presence; it is the very presence of the known to a luminous, immaterial subject. Kaukua’s own reconstruction adds an important nuance: the subject’s presence to itself is the foundational “field of presence,” and the presence of faculties and other objects depends on that more basic self-presence. In Suhrawardi’s terms, consciousness is not one object among the contents of mind. It is the condition under which anything can be present at all.

Discursive thought is therefore secondary, though not unreal. Suhrawardi’s theory of definition says that one truly defines only after one has directly “seen” the thing as it is; demonstration and concept translate a prior apperception into discursive order. He also treats categories like “existence” and “thingness” as mind-dependent considerations rather than ultimate furniture of reality. So the conceptual mind is very real for Suhrawardi, but it is derivative and reconstructive, not fundamental. The deepest layer of mind is presential acquaintance, not concept possession.

4. Perception and intentionality

Suhrawardi’s philosophy of perception is built around vision, which he treats as the paradigm for cognition more broadly. He rejects both extramission and intromission, and he rejects the idea that seeing fundamentally consists in the imprinting of a form in the eye. Instead, visual perception requires an illuminated or illuminable object, a healthy organ, face-to-face encounter, and the absence of a barrier or veil. On the French reading of the Ḥikmat al-Ishrāq, this is the paradigm of iḍāfa ishrāqiyya, an illuminative relation. In the Persian sensory discussion, the same point is pushed in strongly presentational terms: the soul itself establishes a presential, illuminative relation to the object.

The philosophical payoff is large. Perception is not fundamentally the mind’s possession of an inner likeness; it is the object’s light being present, without veil, to a luminous subject. Suhrawardi can therefore be read as giving a kind of presentational intentionality: the object is known in its own locus rather than by way of an internal copy. The SEP formulation is especially helpful here: true vision does not require the acquisition and transfer of forms but the soul’s awareness of the object’s essential light. Physics and metaphysics merge at precisely this point, because the very thing perceived is the object insofar as luminous or manifest.

That said, it would be a mistake to say that Suhrawardi abolishes all representation. Fedor Benevich’s study of Suhrawardi and Avicenna on mental existence is a very useful corrective: according to that reconstruction, what exists in the mind are images or representations of things, not things as such, and the essences of mental objects differ from their extramental counterparts. The best reading, then, is not “no representations ever,” but rather: foundational awareness is non-representational presence, while derivative levels of imagination, memory, and concept use representational contents.

A further non-Anglophone contribution is worth noting. A Persian article argues, on textual grounds, that Suhrawardi’s visual theory should be generalized to the other senses because he says that all the senses return to a single faculty and ultimately to the soul’s own luminous essence. That goes beyond what is stated most explicitly in the visual chapters, but it is a plausible and philosophically important extension: if correct, hearing, touch, and the rest are not separate pipelines feeding a passive mind; they are differentiated modes of one luminous subject’s presence to what is given.

5. Unity of mind, faculties, memory, and imagination

Suhrawardi sharply reduces the autonomy of the Avicennian internal faculties. He criticizes the localization of the inner powers in distinct brain regions because that naturalizes cognition too much and fractures the unity of the subject. The internal faculties become “shadows” or functions of the soul, gathered under a more unified governing light, the Isfahbad-light. The faculty of representation handles particulars, while the governing light perceives universals and immaterial beings. Kaukua’s formulation that the subject’s self-presence grounds the presence of its faculties fits this psychology very well.

Memory and imagination are just as important. Recollection is not primarily the retrieval of traces stored in a brain-like archive; it is, in Suhrawardi’s Platonic idiom, retrieval from a “world of memorial.” Imagination is even more radical. Suhrawardi’s world of suspended images is not just a private theater of subjective pictures: Cambridge scholarship emphasizes that suspended images have an objective rather than merely mental status, and mirrors function not as places where images inhere but as places where they become manifest. Persian work on the imaginal world adds that this fourth world stands between the sensible and the intelligible, and that it is precisely where many “events of the soul” occur.

This imaginal ontology matters directly for philosophy of mind. It allows Suhrawardi to explain dreams, visions, prophetic imagination, miracles, afterlife experience, and the sensible clothing of immaterial truths without reducing them either to crude materialism or to mere inner fantasy. The active imagination receives reflected truths, particularizes universals, and projects them onto common sense in sensible form. That is one reason his symbolic recitals are philosophically serious: they dramatize how mind moves between bodily life, imaginal manifestation, angelic mediation, and divine illumination.

6. Angelic intellects and divine cognition

A faithful Suhrawardian philosophy of mind has to be hierarchical. Human souls, angelic intellects, and divine cognition are not separate subject matters held together only by theology; they are degrees or stations in one illuminative ontology. French scholarship stresses that all pure lights share a common reality and differ by intensity and perfection, while later commentary clarifies Suhrawardi’s distinction between dominating lights and regent or managing lights: the former are bodiless intellectual lights, the latter govern spheres and human beings. Human souls are therefore not isolated minds in a closed human-only domain. They are managing lights within a larger order of mentality.

This is why Suhrawardi’s angelology is philosophically load-bearing. The Iranica account of ʿAql-i Surkh is especially useful here: the archangel in the recital is at once the philosophers’ active intelligence, the angel of the human race, the theologians’ Gabriel or Holy Spirit, and both angel of knowledge and angel of revelation. Iranica also insists that the recitals are not merely about cosmology or even abstract epistemology; they are about the soul’s meeting with the angel and the gradual mastery of doctrine through that encounter. In other words, angelic mind in Suhrawardi is not a theological distraction from philosophy of mind. It is the higher-order cognitive ecology within which human knowing becomes possible and perfected.

If one uses “mind” analogically and carefully, the divine mind is the supreme case in this hierarchy. Suhrawardi’s French commentators emphasize that in the Ḥikmat al-Ishrāq God’s knowledge and vision are not added faculties but are one with divine luminosity and power. The Light of Lights needs no forms in order to know. Nothing is veiled from it; all things are manifest to it. The Persian study of illuminative relation reinforces the point by explaining God’s knowledge of possibles as the presence of all things to God through a dominative relation: creatures are not hidden because they are the concomitants of the divine source. In Kaukua’s reconstruction, the original argumentative home of “knowledge as presence” is precisely the problem of how God knows particulars.

This also explains why divine and angelic “vision” are not merely metaphorical in Suhrawardi. A Persian analysis of his sensory theory argues that for immaterial beings the relevant condition is simply absence of veil: because pure immaterials lack material obstacles, their sight is perfect, and their knowledge is their unveiled manifestation to themselves and to what is other than them. The French study on divine cognition makes the same move from visual perception to presential knowledge. So when Suhrawardi talks about God’s sight or angelic apprehension, he is not dropping out of philosophy into piety; he is extending the same presentational model to its highest cases.

7. Personal identity, survival, and posthumous mind

Suhrawardi’s philosophy of mind is not complete unless one includes survival and afterlife. The rational Isfahbad-light is immortal, but the imaginative dimension of the soul also matters after death, especially for imperfect souls. The independent world of images explains how posthumous pleasures and pains can be genuinely experienced, and why afterlife embodiment must be imaginal rather than grossly material. Persian work on the imaginal world makes the point with unusual clarity: this world explains not only mystical and prophetic experiences but also resurrection, paradise, hell, and “events of the soul.” Suhrawardi’s openness, at least in the Ḥikmat al-Ishrāq, to some unresolved possibility of transmigration for imperfect souls should therefore be treated as part of his larger theory of personal continuity, not as a stray oddity.

8. The extracted philosophy of mind: ten theses
1. The most basic mental fact in Suhrawardi is not representation but self-manifestness: a subject is first present to itself.
2. A genuine mind is a self-subsistent pure light, not merely any luminous thing; physical or accidental lights are not thereby cognizing subjects.
3. Human consciousness is embodied, but embodiment is governance through organs and pneuma, not reduction to bodily processes.
4. Perceptual intentionality is fundamentally presentational: the soul encounters the object in an illuminative relation rather than by receiving a copied form as the deepest layer of explanation.
5. Representation still exists, but at a secondary level: mental images and forms are properties of mind and do not exhaust what knowing is.
6. The mind is more unified than the Avicennian faculty-psychology suggests; the inner faculties are shadows or functions of one governing light.
7. Imagination is ontologically serious. It is not only private picture-making but access to, or commerce with, an objective imaginal order of suspended images.
8. Human cognition is continuous with angelic cognition and analogically with divine cognition. The same logic of luminous self-manifestation explains all three, though with radically different degrees of perfection and power.
9. Ethical and ascetic purification are not external moral add-ons; they alter cognition itself by removing veils and intensifying the soul’s receptivity to higher lights.
10. Personal identity survives bodily loss because the soul is self-subsistent light, and imaginal ontology supplies the medium for posthumous mental continuity and experience.

9. Debates, cautions, and where the evidence is strongest

One major scholarly dispute concerns the original problem to which “knowledge as presence” is addressed. Heidrun Eichner reads it chiefly as a response to problems in the Avicennian mind-body relation, whereas Kaukua argues that its textual point of emergence is the problem of God’s knowledge of particulars in the metaphysical sections of the Talwīḥāt and Mashāriʿ. My own inference is that Kaukua is probably right about the immediate textual setting, while Eichner is right that the concept quickly becomes a general solution to human as well as divine cognition. The evidence points not to an either/or but to a system in which divine knowledge is the original pressure point and human apperception the most accessible analogue.

A second caution concerns sense perception. Suhrawardi is explicit above all about vision, and any extension to hearing or touch must remain interpretive. Still, the Persian argument for generalization is strong because it cites texts where all the senses are said to return to one luminous essence and where the soul, not the organ, is treated as the actual sensor. I would therefore say: extension to all senses is very plausible, but it is still an extrapolation from a theory whose paradigmatic case remains vision.

A third caution concerns anti-representationalism. Modern readers sometimes overstate it. Benevich is right to insist that Suhrawardi still has room for mental images and representational beings. The deeper point is not that representation disappears, but that it ceases to be metaphysically fundamental. The foundational layer of mind is presential awareness; representational contents arise within that prior field.

Finally, one of the more interesting non-Anglophone interpretive moves is the Persian comparison between Suhrawardi’s mature soul-theory and phenomenology. That comparison is illuminating because Suhrawardi does begin from unmediated self-awareness and moves from there to ontology. But it should not be flattened into an anachronism. Suhrawardi’s “phenomenology,” if one wants the word at all, is inseparable from an ontology of lights, a cosmology of angelic intellects, and a doctrine of the Light of Lights.

Bottom line

The most faithful extraction is this: Suhrawardi offers a hierarchical presentational immaterialism. Mind, at its root, is luminous self-manifestness. The human soul is a finite managing light whose self-awareness is immediate, whose perception is fundamentally a matter of unveiled presence, whose imagination opens onto an objective imaginal domain, and whose perfection depends on ethical-spiritual de-veiling. Angelic intellects are not decorative cosmological furniture but higher minds that structure human cognition, and divine cognition is the supreme case in which knowledge, manifestation, and power are one. Remove God and the angels, and the system loses its explanatory summit; remove embodiment, imagination, and afterlife, and it loses its finite psychology. The integrity of Suhrawardi’s philosophy of mind lies in keeping all of those levels together.
